questions.text: I commonly take the initiative to close out projects and declare them complete.
I am attuned to when someone feels embarrassed even if they hide it well.
Even routine goodbyes can stir up a surprising depth of feeling in me.
I prefer flexible timelines that the group agrees upon together.
I find that advocating for someone else's raise or promotion feels as important as pursuing my own.
I find that my capacity for rational, composed analysis during emergencies is the quality that colleagues and friends rely on me for most.
I find that building something I am personally proud of is the strongest motivator I know.
I often have multiple projects going at once and move between them as my interest dictates.
I tend to choose opportunities that advance my personal career over those that primarily benefit others.
I hold the view that pursuing what I want is not at odds with being a good person.
I find that I am more helpful to people in distress when I engage with their problem intellectually rather than emotionally.
I pick up on when the timing of a message is itself the message.
I feel that doing things the same proven way each time ensures quality and reliability.
I can recall specific moments when someone's suffering changed the way I thought about a decision I was making.
I set clear start and end times for work sessions to maintain discipline.
I experience hope as a soaring sensation in my chest, almost like weightlessness.
I feel that ambiguity is a signal for me to step in and create order.
I detect when a person chooses to stand rather than sit as a way to signal they do not intend to stay long.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that best reveals whether a person's ethical framework is serious or superficial.
I think that the ability to suspend emotional reactions during a crisis is a skill that produces better outcomes for everyone.
I tend to move through emotionally charged moments with a sense of groundedness.
I do not usually pick up on the difference between a question that invites discussion and one that shuts it down.
I am the one who cuts off brainstorming to move the group into execution.
I routinely go out of my way to introduce people to opportunities they deserve.
I focus on solving problems rather than navigating the social landscape around them.
I experience deadlines and time pressure as practical challenges rather than sources of physiological stress.
I prefer that the group collectively decides when a meeting has run its course.
I notice that major life events produce a proportionate, not outsized, emotional response in me.
I tend not to detect when a question has a hidden agenda behind it.
I hold the view that a tolerance for mess and ambiguity is a sign of creative flexibility.
I believe every conversation should end with the other person feeling more supported than before.
I prefer to let my day unfold naturally rather than follow a rigid schedule.
I hold the view that giving genuine moral priority to suffering is the most consequential choice I make on a daily basis.
When I get frustrated, the frustration escalates rapidly to a peak.
I would rather slow down and ensure everyone is on board.
I keep a structured record of contacts and when I last communicated with them.
